feedback.text: Hello, as a citizen of the EU I would like to suggest that the Digital Fairness Act cover the area of anti-consumer practices in the video games industry. As already covered by the Stop Killing Games EU Citizen's Initiative, companies are currently using a license distribution model, which they use to revoke access to games, and to make them unplayable at the end of that game's support. What this means is that a consumer may have bought a game, with the expectation of gaining a permanent right to using that product, but that the company may then choose to implement software in the game that makes it only run as long as the company wills it, and then makes it entirely useless. What I would like to suggest is that the Digital Fairness Act restrict future games published within EU jurisdiction from making any bought game unplayable after a purchase. The developers may do this by making the game still functional in its offline features, or even publishing software allowing players to host their own servers for the game. Changing this would not impact currently released games, and would not cause games following the law to force a deficit upon the publishers once it becomes player-hosted. Just as we, as citizens, expect to be able to keep using any service or product we have purchased indefinitely, we expect software and videogames we purchase to not become needlessly unplayable when the developers feel like it.
